Figure XX displays the subject-specific dynamic survival predictions from the full joint model for subjects 10001217, 10001725, 10002428, 10002495, 10003046 and 10003400 over a 5-day prediction horizon from the landmark time. In general, the predicted survival probabilities for most subjects remain above 0.9 throughout the prediction window, with the exception of subject 10003400, which exhibits relatively lower predicted survival probabilities. Among the selected subjects, subject 10003400 shows greater uncertainty in the predicted survival function, as indicated by a wider credible interval. This increased uncertainty may be attributed to greater variability in the biomarker trajectory over time.



```{r fig.pos='H',fig.cap="ROC curve — full covariates joint model (MIMIC-IV)",out.width="70%"}
roc_res <- tvROC(
  jointFit_MIMIC1,
  newdata = newdata_clean,
  Tstart  = 1,
  Dt      = 10
)

sensitivity <- roc_res$TP / (roc_res$TP + roc_res$nFN)
specificity <- roc_res$nTN / (roc_res$nTN + roc_res$FP)

roc_df_MIMIC <- data.frame(
  FPR = 1 - specificity,
  TPR = sensitivity,
  threshold = roc_res$thrs
) %>%
  arrange(FPR)

auc0 <- tvAUC(
  jointFit_MIMIC1,
  newdata = newdata_clean,
  Tstart = 1,
  Dt = 10
)$auc

p_roc <- ggplot(roc_df_MIMIC, aes(x = FPR, y = TPR)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#D6604D", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1,
              linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.65, y = 0.15,
           label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc0, 3)),
           size = 4.5, colour = "#D6604D") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Landmark: 0 years | Horizon: 10 days",
    x        = "1 − Specificity (FPR)",
    y        = "Sensitivity (TPR)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1)

p_roc
```
Figure XX presents the time-dependent ROC curve for the joint model with full covariates, evaluated at a 10-day prediction horizon. The model performance is assessed at a landmark time of 1 time unit, yielding an AUC of 0.803 This indicates that the model has good discriminative ability in distinguishing between patients who experience the event within the specified time window and those who do not. An AUC of 0.803 suggesting reasonable predictive performance of the joint model. Further improvement in predictive accuracy may be achieved by incorporating the full MIMIC-IV dataset, which would increase sample size and improve data balance across time points.



```{r fig.cap="Calibration Curve for Full Joint Model in MIMIC-IV data"}

landmark_times = c(0, 5, 10)

newdata_clean <- newdata_clean %>%
  group_by(subject_id) %>%
  arrange(time_c, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  ungroup()

newdata_clean <- newdata_clean %>%
  distinct(subject_id, time_c, .keep_all = TRUE)

tvBrier_result_full <- lapply(landmark_times, function(t0) {
  tvBrier(
    jointFit_MIMIC1,
    newdata = newdata_clean,
    Tstart = t0,
    Dt = 5
  )

})

extract_brier <- function(res, t0) {

  data.frame(
    landmark = t0,
    horizon = res$Thoriz,
    brier = res$Brier
  )
}

tvBrier_full_df <- lapply(seq_along(landmark_times), function(i) {

  extract_brier(
    tvBrier_result_full[[i]],
    landmark_times[i]
  )

}) %>% dplyr::bind_rows()

knitr::kable(tvBrier_full_df, caption = "Time Dependent Brier Score for Full Joint Model",digits=3)

grid <- seq(
  0,
  ceiling(max(biomarkersP_icu_clean$Time_alive, na.rm = TRUE)),
  by = 5
)

event_table <-  biomarkersP_icu_clean %>%
     group_by(subject_id) %>%
     summarise(
         Time_alive = first(Time_alive),
         event = max(death_event),
         .groups = "drop"
     ) %>%
     mutate(interval = cut(Time_alive, breaks = grid <- grid, right = FALSE)) %>%
     group_by(interval) %>%
     summarise(
         n_events = sum(event),
         n_total  = n(),
         event_rate = n_events / n_total
     )

knitr::kable(event_table, caption = "Distribution of death events in 1-unit time intervals",digits=3)
```
The landmark times for the time-dependent Brier score are selected in a data-driven manner based on the distribution of observed event times within the study period. Table XX presents the Brier score of the full joint model at different prediction horizons following selected landmark times. The Brier score remains low at early prediction horizons (0–5 days), indicating strong short-term predictive accuracy. An increase in the Brier score is observed at the 5–10 day horizon, which may be attributed to a reduced number of events and increased uncertainty in survival prediction. Although the lowest Brier score is observed at the 10–15 day horizon, this result should be interpreted with caution due to the small number of individuals and uneven event distribution in this interval, as shown in Table XX


```{r fig.cap="Calibration plot for full joint model"}
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
par(mar = c(4,4,2,1))

calibration_plot(
  jointFit_MIMIC1,
  newdata = newdata_clean,
  Tstart = 1,
  Dt = 10
)
```
The calibration curve compares the predicted survival probabilities with the observed survival proportions at specified landmark times and prediction horizons. Based on Figure XX, it shows that the the model tends to overestimate survival probabilities, as the calibration curve lies slighlt below 45 degrees reference line.

## Conclusion
